content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

September 5, 2006

Janyth C. Bolden, RRT, RCP
President
The California Society for
Respiratory Care
1961 Main Street, Suite #246
Watsonville, California 95076

RE: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-06-159

Dear Ms. Bolden:

This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of the California
Society for Respiratory Care regarding the "personal use of campaign funds" provisions
of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTION

May travel and lodging expenses associated with attending meetings of the
California Society for Respiratory Care ("CSRC") with the goal of effecting regulatory
and statutory changes concerning the respiratory care profession be paid for newly-
appointed members of the Advance Practice Commission out of the campaign funds of
the California Society for Respiratory Care Political Action Committee, a sponsored
general purpose recipient committee?

CONCLUSION

Since the proposed expenditures will be limited to providing transportation and
lodging to Advance Practice Commission members for the sole purpose of developing
strategy and collecting data to effect state regulatory and statutory changes, these
expenditures would be a permitted use of committee funds.

_________________
¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-06-159
Page No. 2

FACTS

CSRC is a nonprofit membership organization. Members have the option of
paying a voluntary contribution to the organization's sponsored committee.

CSRC's board of directors voted to investigate the feasibility of raising the entry
level of the profession. A committee was formed, named "Advance Practice
Commission," to further this goal. These committee members would attend the three to
four annual meetings of CSRC's board of directors, which meets in Berkeley,
Sacramento, Los Angeles, and San Diego throughout the year. The committee members
are not on the CSRC board and would not otherwise attend these meetings; their
attendance will be specifically to work on this proposed project. The end result of the
project would be for the organization to attempt to change legislation and regulations
concerning the respiratory care profession.

In a telephone conversation of August 24, 2006, you stated that CSRC does not
pay its members per diem; the only expenses you are proposing to be paid for out of
committee funds would be for committee members' travel and, if necessary, lodging.
You stated that individual members are personally responsible for paying for their food
and other expenses.

ANALYSIS

The Act governs the expenditure of funds held by a committee, such as the
California Society for Respiratory Care Political Action Committee. Section 89512.5
specifically requires any expenditure made by a committee not controlled by a candidate
to be reasonably related to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose. However,
section 89513(a) further specifies that when any individual with authority to approve the
expenditure of campaign funds held by a committee are used to pay travel expenses and
necessary accommodations, the expenditures must be directly related to a political,
legislative, or governmental purpose.

Section 89513(a)(1) provides that:

"… payments or reimbursements for travel and necessary
accommodations shall be considered as directly related to a political,
legislative, or governmental purpose if the payments would meet standards
similar to the standards of the Internal Revenue Service pursuant to
Sections 162 and 274 of the Internal Revenue Code for deductions of
travel expenses under the federal income tax law."2

2 Internal Revenue Code section 162(a)(2) sets forth general rules that permit the deduction of all
ordinary and necessary traveling expenses while away from home in the pursuit of a trade or business.
Pursuant to this section, campaign funds may be used for "business portions" of the travel. To the extent

File No. A-06-159
Page No. 3


For analysis of this section of federal law, you may contact the Internal Revenue Service
at (877) 829-5500. This telephone number is reserved to assist non-profit organizations
with federal tax questions.

If none of the members of the Advance Practice Commission, or anyone else
whose expenses will be paid from committee funds, has the authority to approve
committee expenditures, the expenditures need only meet the criteria of being reasonably
related to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose. The development of strategy
and gathering of information for the purpose of influencing or attempting to influence
state administrative or legislative action appear to meet these criteria. (Kaufman Advice
Letter, No. A-96-175.)

If any one of the participants whose travel is proposed to be paid out of committee
funds is authorized to approve the expenditures of the committee and if the travel
expenses do not meet the criteria set forth in the Internal Revenue Code sections
referenced above, the expenditures must be directly related to a political, legislative, or
governmental purpose. Again, it appears that the work involved in the proposed project
would meet these criteria. Thus, with the facts you have provided, the proposed travel
and necessary accommodations may be paid out of committee funds.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: Kevin S. Moen, PhD
Political Reform Consultant
Technical Assistance Division

that any day is not a business day (such as days on which there are no meetings or formal activities),
campaign funds may not be used.
